refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications for all captured elements, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent active work zones and restricted areas.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite model data for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation; decouple acquisition method from representation by mapping sensor outputs to schema-compliant entities.

DP5: Delegate spatial feature capture to external specialized service (e.g., surveying, photogrammetry, LiDAR provider); ingest delivered model, validate against operational safety/planning requirements, and adapt to internal representation standard.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 (with DP1 as complementary realization): Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation; decouple acquisition method from representation by mapping sensor outputs to schema-compliant entities. Pair with multi-modal sensing fusion (DP1) to acquire and normalize raw observations into schema-compliant spatial features.

potential_sources: Schema evolution risk: Changes to feature schema (e.g., new safety attribute types) may require re-acquisition or re-processing of historical data if not versioned.
Sensor-to-schema mapping brittleness: If sensor calibration or classification logic drifts, schema validation may reject valid observations; requires monitoring and feedback loops.

Downstream consumer coupling: Planning/execution functions may make implicit assumptions about feature completeness or accuracy; schema should explicitly declare coverage guarantees and uncertainty bounds.

Spatial registration coupling: If multi-modal fusion (DP1) fails to achieve required coordinate frame alignment, schema validation alone cannot recover; requires tight integration with acquisition pipeline.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 + DP1 combination achieves good separation of concerns: schema defines the contract (what features must be captured and how they are represented), while DP1 defines the mechanism (how to acquire and fuse observations). This allows schema evolution independent of sensor technology, and sensor upgrades independent of downstream consumers. Coupling is primarily at the schema-to-acquisition boundary (sensor-to-schema mapping) and at the schema-to-consumer boundary (feature completeness guarantees).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable and can be encoded into feature schema (e.g., safety zone definitions, hazard classifications). Risk: If policies change frequently or are context-dependent, schema becomes a bottleneck; mitigation: design schema with policy-parameterization (e.g., safety zones as configurable attributes, not hard-coded classes).

Assumption: Multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) is sufficient to capture all required features with acceptable accuracy and coverage. Risk: Occlusions, dynamic objects, or features not directly observable (e.g., subsurface utilities) may be missed; mitigation: define explicit coverage and uncertainty bounds in schema; consider hybrid acquisition (sensor + manual annotation).

Assumption: Spatial registration across multiple sensors/zones can be achieved to required precision. Risk: GPS denial, magnetic interference, or accumulated drift in inertial fusion may degrade coordinate frame quality; mitigation: design schema to support local coordinate frames and relative geometry; provide registration confidence metadata.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can consume a unified spatial model and do not require real-time updates. Risk: If worksite changes dynamically (e.g., equipment repositioning, temporary hazards), model staleness may cause safety/efficiency issues; mitigation: define update frequency and latency requirements; consider streaming change notifications.

Assumption: Feature classification (e.g., hazard type, operational zone) can be automated or is provided by external source. Risk: Misclassification or incomplete labeling may propagate to planning/execution; mitigation: require human validation for safety-critical features; design schema to flag low-confidence classifications.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria (model generation and availability) are achievable with proposed DP. Risk: If acquisition/fusion/validation pipeline is too slow or unreliable, model may not be available when planning/execution needs it; mitigation: define SLAs for model freshness and availability; design for graceful degradation (e.g., use cached model if real-time update fails).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types and their attributes (geometry, safety classification, operational constraints); (2) Spatial accuracy and coverage requirements (e.g., ±0.5m horizontal, 95% feature detection rate); (3) Update frequency and latency SLAs (e.g., model refreshed every 5 minutes, available within 30s of request); (4) Handling of dynamic features and uncertainty (e.g., how to represent temporary hazards, confidence bounds); (5) Integration points with planning/execution (e.g., query interface, change notification mechanism). This will enable tighter evaluation of DP feasibility and clearer definition of IM boundaries (e.g., schema validator module, sensor fusion module, spatial database module, query service module).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for declarative feature schema definition, schema validation enforcement, sensor-to-schema mapping, and multi-modal sensing fusion for safety-critical spatial feature representation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw sensor observations (multi-modal: LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for fusion and schema mapping.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits schema-validated spatial features and safety constraint violations to downstream safety decision logic and Vehicle control.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational constraint specifications and geometry primitive definitions to configure schema validation rules dynamically.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of schema definition, validation, and constraint enforcement. Multi-modal fusion and sensor normalization are safety-critical functions that belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry input and SafetyAlert output cross boundaries; schema logic remains encapsulated.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/control entity, not a data representation and validation layer. Schema validation and fusion logic would diffuse across Vehicle control, sensor drivers, and state management. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/feedback channel, not a schema or validation authority. Placing schema logic here would couple operator interface to safety-critical representation, creating maintenance and safety verification burden.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level container. While it could host Safety Services, it is too broad to serve as the direct IM boundary. Schema and fusion logic must be owned by a specific, focused module (Safety Services) within NGHE for clear accountability and testability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP4 + DP1. It owns the declarative schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints), enforces validation conformance, and implements multi-modal fusion. This centralizes safety-critical representation logic and decouples sensor acquisition (Vehicle/Teleoperation) from schema compliance (Safety Services), reducing diffusion and improving auditability.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services does not currently have direct access to raw Telemetry streams from all sensor modalities, integration effort may be required to wire multi-modal inputs.
Risk: Schema evolution (adding new geometry primitives or safety attributes) must be coordinated across Safety Services and all consumers of SafetyAlert; versioning discipline is essential.

Risk: Real-time fusion performance (latency of schema validation and normalization) must be validated against safety-critical timing requirements; if Safety Services becomes a bottleneck, consider co-locating fusion logic with Vehicle sensor drivers while keeping schema definition and validation in Safety Services.

Risk: Decoupling sensor acquisition from representation assumes a clean mapping interface; if sensor modalities have conflicting or ambiguous outputs, the mapping logic may become complex and require domain expertise not naturally resident in Safety Services.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 + DP1 directly addresses FR by establishing a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforcing capture/representation conformance through validation. The pairing of schema definition with multi-modal sensing fusion decouples acquisition method from representation, satisfying the requirement to capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable form. Logic is sound and complete.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Multi-modal sensor input routing. If raw Telemetry streams from LiDAR, camera, radar, and IMU are not currently wired directly to Safety Services, integration effort and potential latency overhead may arise. Verify that Telemetry interface can deliver all modalities with sufficient bandwidth and timing guarantees.

Risk: Schema evolution coordination. Adding new geometry primitives or safety attributes requires versioning discipline across Safety Services (schema owner) and all SafetyAlert consumers (Vehicle control, planning/execution). Lack of formal versioning protocol could cause silent incompatibilities.

Risk: Real-time fusion bottleneck. Schema validation and multi-modal normalization are computationally intensive. If Safety Services becomes a latency bottleneck, safety-critical timing requirements may be violated. Mitigation: validate end-to-end latency against acceptance criteria; consider co-locating fusion preprocessing with Vehicle sensor drivers while keeping schema definition and validation in Safety Services.

Risk: Sensor modality ambiguity. If LiDAR, camera, radar, and IMU outputs have conflicting or ambiguous interpretations (e.g., occlusion handling, coordinate frame alignment), the sensor-to-schema mapping logic may become complex and require domain expertise not naturally resident in Safety Services. Recommend early collaboration with sensor engineering and safety domain experts to define unambiguous mapping rules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08). It is the natural owner of declarative schema definition, validation enforcement, and multi-modal fusion—all safety-critical functions. Interfaces are clean: Telemetry (in) for raw sensor data, SafetyAlert (out) for validated features, Tasking (in) for dynamic constraint configuration. Schema logic remains encapsulated within Safety Services, minimizing cross-module diffusion. Vehicle and Teleoperation are correctly excluded (poor separation of concerns). NGHE Solution is too broad; Safety Services is the appropriate specific boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require that 'a worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.' Safety Services' SafetyAlert output (schema-validated spatial features) directly satisfies this requirement. Verification can be performed by: (1) confirming that SafetyAlert emits geometry primitives and safety attributes matching the agreed feature set; (2) validating that planning/execution functions receive and consume these features; (3) testing schema validation against worksite safety plan and operational policies. Traceability is clear: FR -> DP4/DP1 -> Safety Services IM -> SafetyAlert interface -> planning/execution consumption.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services is explicitly allocated ownership of schema definition, validation, and fusion. Responsibilities are well-defined: (1) define and maintain declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints); (2) enforce schema conformance through validation; (3) map multi-modal sensor outputs to schema-compliant entities; (4) emit validated features via SafetyAlert. Ownership is unambiguous, and the module boundary is cohesive. Coordination points (Telemetry input, Tasking input, SafetyAlert output) are explicit and testable.

recommended_revisions: Establish formal sensor-to-schema mapping specification. Document unambiguous rules for converting LiDAR, camera, radar, and IMU outputs to geometry primitives and safety attributes. Include handling of sensor conflicts, occlusion, coordinate frame alignment, and uncertainty quantification. Assign ownership to Safety Services with input from sensor engineering and safety domain experts.

Define schema versioning and evolution protocol. Establish a formal process for adding/modifying geometry primitives and safety attributes, including version numbering, backward compatibility rules, and notification mechanism for SafetyAlert consumers. Ensure planning/execution functions can handle schema version changes gracefully.

Validate real-time fusion latency. Measure end-to-end latency from raw sensor input (Telemetry) to validated feature output (SafetyAlert) under nominal and worst-case conditions. Confirm that latency meets safety-critical timing requirements. If latency exceeds acceptable bounds, consider architectural optimization (e.g., preprocessing fusion in Vehicle sensor drivers, keeping validation in Safety Services).

Confirm Telemetry interface completeness. Verify that the Telemetry interface from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems delivers all required sensor modalities (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) with sufficient fidelity, bandwidth, and synchronization for multi-modal fusion. Document any gaps or integration work required.

Specify agreed feature set for worksite model. Collaborate with planning/execution functions and safety stakeholders to formally define the set of geometry primitives, safety attributes, and operational constraints that constitute the 'agreed set of site features' referenced in acceptance criteria. Document this as a schema specification artifact owned by Safety Services.

Establish SafetyAlert consumer contract. Define the interface contract (message format, timing guarantees, error handling) between Safety Services (SafetyAlert producer) and planning/execution functions (consumers). Ensure consumers can validate and handle schema-compliant features reliably.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The logic is sound: DP4 (declarative feature schema) + DP1 (multi-modal sensing fusion) directly address FR1 (capture and represent worksite spatial features). Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary with clear ownership and cohesive responsibilities. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that critical implementation details—sensor-to-schema mapping, schema versioning, real-time latency validation, and consumer contracts—are not yet decomposed into actionable sub-requirements. Given high safety criticality, these details must be explicitly derived as next-level FRs to ensure completeness and traceability before detailed design proceeds. Stopping here would leave architectural and integration risks unresolved.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Define and maintain unambiguous sensor-to-schema mapping rules that convert LiDAR, camera, radar, and IMU outputs to geometry primitives and safety attributes, including handling of sensor conflicts, occlusion, coordinate frame alignment, and uncertainty quantification.

FR1.2: Establish and enforce a formal schema versioning and evolution protocol that manages additions/modifications to geometry primitives and safety attributes, ensures backward compatibility, and notifies SafetyAlert consumers of schema changes.
FR1.3: Validate that end-to-end latency from raw sensor input (Telemetry) to validated feature output (SafetyAlert) meets safety-critical timing requirements under nominal and worst-case conditions.
FR1.4: Confirm that the Telemetry interface delivers all required sensor modalities (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) with sufficient fidelity, bandwidth, and synchronization to support multi-modal fusion.

FR1.5: Formally specify the agreed set of geometry primitives, safety attributes, and operational constraints that constitute the worksite model, in collaboration with planning/execution and safety stakeholders.

FR1.6: Define and enforce the SafetyAlert interface contract (message format, timing guarantees, error handling) between Safety Services and planning/execution consumers to ensure reliable feature consumption.

assumptions: Safety Services is the correct IM boundary for schema definition, validation, and multi-modal fusion (containment_score 0.92).
Telemetry interface can be extended to deliver all required sensor modalities with sufficient bandwidth and timing guarantees.
Planning/execution functions are capable of consuming schema-compliant features and handling schema version changes.
Sensor engineering and safety domain experts are available to collaborate on sensor-to-schema mapping rules.
Real-time fusion latency can be measured and validated against acceptance criteria before production deployment.
open_risks: Multi-modal sensor input routing: Raw Telemetry streams may not currently be wired directly to Safety Services; integration effort and latency overhead may arise.
Schema evolution coordination: Lack of formal versioning protocol could cause silent incompatibilities between Safety Services and SafetyAlert consumers.

Real-time fusion bottleneck: Schema validation and multi-modal normalization may exceed safety-critical timing requirements; architectural optimization (e.g., preprocessing in Vehicle sensor drivers) may be needed.

Sensor modality ambiguity: Conflicting or ambiguous interpretations of LiDAR, camera, radar, and IMU outputs (occlusion, coordinate frame alignment) may require domain expertise not naturally resident in Safety Services.
Agreed feature set clarity: The 'agreed set of site features' referenced in acceptance criteria is not yet formally specified; ambiguity could lead to incomplete or incorrect schema definition.
evidence_needed: Formal sensor-to-schema mapping specification document with unambiguous rules for all sensor modalities and conflict resolution strategies.
Schema versioning protocol document with version numbering scheme, backward compatibility rules, and consumer notification mechanism.
End-to-end latency measurement data (nominal and worst-case) confirming compliance with safety-critical timing requirements.
Telemetry interface specification confirming delivery of all required sensor modalities with fidelity, bandwidth, and synchronization guarantees.
Agreed feature set specification document (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) signed off by planning/execution and safety stakeholders.
SafetyAlert interface contract specification (message format, timing guarantees, error handling) agreed upon by Safety Services and consumer functions.
Collaboration records (design reviews, domain expert input) documenting sensor engineering and safety domain expertise applied to mapping and versioning decisions.
